nativeRange.habitat: Shallow sub-tidal coral reef lagoons and turbid inshore waters; tends to settle on dead corals and rock surfaces, either solitary or in small clusters

decorPreferences: ample open space around disc — no close coral neighbors

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae, but actively carnivorous. In the wild, lures fish with an attracting scent, then rapidly folds its disc into a closed bag to trap and digest prey — a response that reportedly completes in under three seconds. In captivity, target-feeding a small piece of meaty food (fish flesh, clam, shrimp) near the central mouth weekly will generally trigger a feeding response. Monthly feeding is sometimes cited, but weekly is consistent with the controller's guidance for this predatory species.

goodWith: large sessile invertebrates that cannot be engulfed
avoidWith: small fish (especially clownfish — may be attracted and engulfed)
any mobile invertebrate small enough to be trapped by the disc

summary: A large, predatory corallimorpharian capable of engulfing small fish and shrimp by folding its disc into a closed bag. Generally easy to keep but requires a carefully chosen tankmate list; best suited to coral-only or fish-free systems.
careNotes:
Amplexidiscus fenestrafer is the largest commonly kept corallimorpharian and one of the few corals considered genuinely dangerous to fish and shrimp. Despite looking like a passive disc, it is an active predator: it is reported to release a chemical attractant that lures fish (particularly clownfish, which may mistake it for a host anemone), then rapidly contracts its lobed edges inward — forming a closed, balloon-like sac from which escape is essentially impossible. This action has been documented completing in under three seconds.

Husbandry notes:
• Lighting: Low to moderate intensity is generally advised (approximately 40–100 PAR). Unlike SPS corals, this species tends to bleach under intense light and does best in shaded or lower-lit positions.
• Flow: Low, gentle flow is recommended. Direct laminar flow or powerhead blasts should be avoided — the disc should remain open and relaxed, not folded.
• Placement: Place at or near substrate level with ample open space surrounding the disc. The disc can expand to 30–45 cm across in mature specimens; neighboring corals placed too close will be stung or engulfed during feeding events.
• Feeding: Target-feed meaty foods (fish flesh, clam, shrimp, squid) near the central mouth weekly. Food contact with the disc surface triggers the closing response. In the wild it is piscivorous; in captivity dead meaty foods generally elicit the same response.
• Tankmate safety: Considered high-risk to all small-to-medium fish and mobile invertebrates (shrimp, crabs, small snails). Newly introduced fish, fish sleeping on the substrate, and any specimen that wanders across the disc surface are particularly at risk. Most experienced keepers house this species in a coral-only or large-fish-only system. Clownfish should specifically be excluded — they are apparently attracted rather than repelled by the chemical cues this coral emits.
• Reproduction: Reproduces primarily via asexual longitudinal fission (splitting); pedal laceration and budding also reported.
• Note on common name: 'Elephant Ear Mushroom' is also applied in the trade to Rhodactis mussoides (LiveAquaria product 657), which is a smaller, non-predatory species with substantially different compatibility requirements. Amplexidiscus fenestrafer is the larger, predatory species and should not be confused with it. When purchasing, verify the scientific name.
breedingNotes: Reproduces readily in captivity via asexual fission — the disc gradually splits longitudinally into two daughter polyps. Pedal laceration (small tissue pieces left on the substrate that develop into new polyps) and occasional budding have also been reported. Sexual reproduction is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: Tropical Fish Hobbyist Magazine — Amplexidiscus fenestrafer
primary.notes: Species-specific article covering taxonomy, native habitat, size, water parameters (temperature 72–83°F, SG 1.025, pH 8.1–8.2, alkalinity 2.5 meq/L, Ca 380–430 ppm), lighting, flow, feeding, and predatory behavior in detail.

additional.name: AquariumDomain — Giant Cup Mushroom Coral Species Profile
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (12+ in), temperature (72–78°F), salinity (1.023–1.025), pH (8.1–8.4), alkalinity (8–12 dKH), care level (Easy), lighting (Medium), flow (Low to Moderate), and predatory behavior. Temperature upper bound of 83°F (TFH) vs 78°F (AquariumDomain) — averaged to 28°C max.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Elephant Ear Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (46 cm / 18 in), temperature (22–28°C), salinity (1.024–1.026), pH (8.1–8.4), predatory mechanism description, and recommended coral-only setup. Care level listed as 'specialist aquarium only'; averaged with two 'Easy' sources to 'intermediate' per controller's brief.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Amplexidiscus fenestrafer
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Order Corallimorpharia, Family Discosomatidae), depth range (5–25 m), size (40 cm), and native range (Indo-West Pacific). Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: LiveAquaria — Elephant Ear Mushroom (Rhodactis mussoides)
additional.notes: LiveAquaria's 'Elephant Ear Mushroom' product page is for Rhodactis mussoides, not Amplexidiscus fenestrafer. General mushroom coral parameters (temp 72–78°F, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12) were used only to cross-check broad water parameter ranges. Husbandry and compatibility data for Amplexidiscus fenestrafer were taken from species-specific sources above.
